questions.question: What is most closely a central idea of Vonnegut's letter to the students of Xavier High School?

responses.content: D. Don't allow the events of one day to define the rest of your life.

questions.question: Which sentence from the text most strongly supports the correct answer to the previous question?

questions.question: In the same box, write the rest of your body paragraph, building on your topic sentence. Your paragraph should include at least two pieces of evidence, and an explanation of how each piece of evidence develops the main idea. Be sure to end your paragraph with a concluding sentence.

responses.content: The main Idea that the author develops about Barbara Johns Is that she Is also brave, smart, and dedicated just like Claudette Colvin. The reason that she Is dedicated and brave Is because she planned a whole entire ploy so she cant stay at her school. She forged a signature for a assembly and also because she made sure that people would protest for a assembly so they could finally get a new school instead of the horrible one they are In. The proof for this Is In paragraph 6, and It says "For six months, the students planned their strategy. They put it into effect on April 23, 1951. Late that morning, Moton’s principal, Boyd Jones, received a phone call from one of the conspirators, advising him that two of his students were about to be arrested by police at the Greyhound bus station. Jones left school in a hurry, never suspecting that the summons was a ploy to get him safely away so that a note with Jones’s forged signature could be sent to each classroom, announcing an immediate school assembly. After the teachers and the school’s 450 students gathered in the auditorium, the curtains on the stage parted and revealed Barbara Johns, not Boyd Jones, standing at the podium. She declared that the meeting was for students only and asked the teachers to leave. When some teachers protested, Johns removed one of her shoes and smacked it on a bench, “I want you out of here!” she shouted. At that, all the teachers left, some with student escorts. Then Johns got down to business. The time had come, she said, for dramatic action. Joined by the other student organizers, she called for a student strike and produced hand-lettered picket signs proclaiming “We Want a New School or None at All” and “Down with the Tar-Paper Shacks.” The students cheered and marched out of the auditorium, with Barbara Johns in the vanguard." This shows that she Is brave, smart, and dedicated because of all the things that she has done to get a new school.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response clearly identifies two or more traits (brave, smart, dedicated) and connects them to Barbara Johns' actions. It interprets the significance of her actions (planning, forging a signature, leading a protest) and uses evidence from the text to support the claim. The response is a complete sentence and aligns with the main idea about Barbara Johns' leadership and courage. The language and reasoning are appropriate for a fourth-grade level, and the answer goes beyond just listing events by explaining what they show about Barbara Johns.

responses.content:
The main Idea that the author develops about Barbara Johns Is that she Is also brave, smart, and dedicated just like Claudette Colvin. The reason that she Is dedicated and brave Is because she planned a whole entire ploy so she cant stay at her school. She forged a signature for a assembly and also because she made sure that people would protest for a assembly so they could finally get a new school instead of the horrible one they are In. The proof for this Is In paragraph 6, and It says "For six months, the students planned their strategy. They put it into effect on April 23, 1951. Late that morning, Moton’s principal, Boyd Jones, received a phone call from one of the conspirators, advising him that two of his students were about to be arrested by police at the Greyhound bus station. Jones left school in a hurry, never suspecting that the summons was a ploy to get him safely away so that a note with Jones’s forged signature could be sent to each classroom, announcing an immediate school assembly. After the teachers and the school’s 450 students gathered in the auditorium, the curtains on the stage parted and revealed Barbara Johns, not Boyd Jones, standing at the podium. She declared that the meeting was for students only and asked the teachers to leave. When some teachers protested, Johns removed one of her shoes and smacked it on a bench, “I want you out of here!” she shouted. At that, all the teachers left, some with student escorts. Then Johns got down to business. The time had come, she said, for dramatic action. Joined by the other student organizers, she called for a student strike and produced hand-lettered picket signs proclaiming “We Want a New School or None at All” and “Down with the Tar-Paper Shacks.” The students cheered and marched out of the auditorium, with Barbara Johns in the vanguard."  And also on paragraph 7, It shows that they actually responded because of Barbara. It says In paragraph 7 "The strike leaders sent a letter to the NAACP in Richmond that same afternoon. “We hate to impose as we are doing,” the letter began, “but under the circumstances we are facing, we have to ask for your help.” Three days later, two top civil rights lawyers were on their way to Farmville. By this time, the NAACP had already decided to challenge “separate but equal” in favor of total desegregation. Although there may have been, as Richard Kluger has written, “no less promising place in all Virginia to wage the fight for equal schools,” the NAACP lawyers were willing to try. In a shabby basement meeting room of a local church, they told the students and some parents that the NAACP would help them only if they were prepared to go for broke—desegregation or nothing—and then only if the students could demonstrate that they had the support of the adults in the black community. The strikers were stunned but enthusiastic. “It seemed like reaching for the moon,” said Barbara Johns."
This shows that she Is brave, smart, and dedicated because of all the things that she has done to get a new school.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response includes a clear topic sentence and two pieces of evidence from the text, but it only provides one general explanation at the end that covers both pieces of evidence together. It says In paragraph 7 "The strike leaders sent a letter to the NAACP in Richmond that same afternoon. “We hate to impose as we are doing,” the letter began, “but under the circumstances we are facing, we have to ask for your help.” Three days later, two top civil rights lawyers were on their way to Farmville. By this time, the NAACP had already decided to challenge “separate but equal” in favor of total desegregation. Although there may have been, as Richard Kluger has written, “no less promising place in all Virginia to wage the fight for equal schools,” the NAACP lawyers were willing to try. In a shabby basement meeting room of a local church, they told the students and some parents that the NAACP would help them only if they were prepared to go for broke—desegregation or nothing—and then only if the students could demonstrate that they had the support of the adults in the black community. The strikers were stunned but enthusiastic. “It seemed like reaching for the moon,” said Barbara Johns."
This shows that she Is brave, smart, and dedicated because of all the things that she has done to get a new school.
In Conclusion, this shows that both Claudette Colvin and Barbara Johns are smart, dedicated, and brave for the things that they did for the community who got mistreated for their color like making the white people start to be equal with them, getting new schools so black people wouldn't be mistreated, and way more much things that helped people who got mistreated.
responses.bucket_rationale: The paragraph contains spelling errors such as 'Civiil' instead of 'Civil' and 'Calvin's' instead of 'Colvin's'. These are identifiable spelling mistakes, which classify the response as 'incorrect'.
